title: Whether old apartment buildings should be allowed to keep their patina
summary: Argue whether ageing residential facades — stained, faded, weather-marked — should be preserved as they are or restored to a uniform freshness.
topic: Whether peeling paint and weather-stained walls in old apartment districts deserve preservation
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether old residential buildings in cities such as Lisbon, Hanoi, or Naples should be permitted to keep their faded, stained, weather-marked exteriors — or whether owners and councils should restore them to clean, uniform colour. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one named district, street, or policy. Take a clear position rather than balancing both sides.
hintStarters: What a freshly painted facade quietly erases is the long, accumulated record of…
Admittedly, peeling render and rust streaks can shade into genuine structural neglect, and a wall left untreated for…
Walking through the back streets of the Alfama district in Lisbon, one notices that the buildings most loved by residents are rarely the…

hintVocab.meaningVi: mặt tiền đã bị thời tiết bào mòn
lớp gỉ / dấu vết thời gian trên bề mặt

hintVocab.phrase: to restore something to a uniform finish
hintVocab.meaningVi: khôi phục về bề mặt đồng nhất

hintVocab.phrase: to be flattened into a postcard
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị làm phẳng thành một bưu thiếp
cảnh quan đường phố đồng nhất hóa
kết cấu của thời gian được sống qua
kế thừa lịch sử của một tòa nhà

sampleText:
A freshly painted wall is, in some sense, a wall without a history. It has no record of the winter the rain came in sideways, no trace of the family who once lived behind the second window, no memory of the political poster that was glued there in 1974 and only partly scraped off. What a uniformly restored facade quietly erases is precisely this long, accumulated record — the texture of lived time pressed into the render.

Nowhere is this loss more visible than in the older neighbourhoods of southern European cities. Walking through the Alfama district in Lisbon, one notices that the buildings most loved by residents and visitors alike are rarely the recently restored ones. The faded blues and ochres, the cracked azulejo, the rust streaks running from old iron balcony brackets — all of this is, technically, deterioration. It is also the visual record of a working-class neighbourhood that has refused to be flattened into a postcard.

Admittedly, the line between graceful weathering and genuine structural neglect is not always easy to draw. A facade left untreated for forty years can shade from patina into hazard: loose render can fall on the street below, rusted ironwork can fail, peeling paint can hide damp that is eating into the masonry behind it. Residents have a right to safe buildings, and councils have a duty to enforce that right. Nobody who lives beneath a crumbling cornice is interested in its civic memory.

Even so, the response to this risk does not have to be the wholesale repainting of entire districts in approved tones. What sensitive heritage policy looks like, in practice, is something narrower: structural repair where it is genuinely needed, cleaning rather than overpainting where surfaces are sound, and a willingness to leave the marks of weather where those marks are doing no harm. Naples has experimented with exactly this approach in parts of the Spanish Quarter, funding facade-stabilisation grants that explicitly do not require owners to restore the original colour.

Granted, some residents would prefer a cleaner street. The desire to live behind a freshly rendered facade is not snobbery; it is, often enough, a perfectly reasonable wish to feel that one's home is cared for. The mistake is to assume that care necessarily means erasure.

A city that retouches every wall into invisibility is one that has confused maintenance with cosmetic surgery. Districts whose walls are allowed to age in public retain something that the homogenised streetscape cannot offer: a way of seeing the past without having to visit a museum.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to redefine what restoration costs
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a uniformly restored facade quietly erases is precisely this long, accumulated record' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để định nghĩa lại điều bị mất khi phục dựng. Trạng từ 'quietly' và 'precisely' nén toàn bộ luận điểm vào một câu đơn duy nhất, và phần sau 'is' chứa nội dung quan trọng nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a uniformly restored facade quietly erases is precisely this long, accumulated record' uses a what-cleft to redefine what restoration actually costs. The adverbs 'quietly' and 'precisely' compress the entire argument into a single sentence, and the most important content sits in the emphatic position after 'is'.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Reduced relative clause and passive participle for dense description
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A facade left untreated for forty years can shade from patina into hazard' dùng mệnh đề quan hệ rút gọn ở dạng bị động ('left untreated for forty years') để nén toàn bộ ý 'một mặt tiền mà người ta đã không bảo trì trong bốn mươi năm' vào ba từ. Cách viết này cho phép câu có chủ ngữ dài, mô tả và thông tin mà không cần đến mệnh đề quan hệ đầy đủ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A facade left untreated for forty years can shade from patina into hazard' uses a reduced passive relative clause ('left untreated for forty years') to compress 'a facade that no one has treated for forty years' into three words. This lets the subject carry a long descriptive load without needing a full relative clause.

sampleTranslationVi:
Một bức tường mới sơn, theo một nghĩa nào đó, là một bức tường không có lịch sử. Nó không có ghi chép về mùa đông mưa tạt vào, không còn dấu vết của gia đình từng sống sau ô cửa sổ thứ hai, không còn ký ức về tấm áp phích chính trị đã được dán ở đó vào năm 1974 và chỉ được cạo đi một phần. Điều mà một mặt tiền được khôi phục đồng nhất âm thầm xóa bỏ chính là cái ghi chép dài và tích lũy này — kết cấu của thời gian được sống qua, in dấu vào lớp vữa.

Không đâu mất mát này lại hiển hiện rõ hơn ở các khu phố cổ của các thành phố Nam Âu. Đi bộ qua khu Alfama ở Lisbon, người ta nhận thấy rằng những tòa nhà được cư dân và du khách yêu thích nhất hiếm khi là những tòa nhà mới được phục dựng. Những màu xanh lam và màu hoàng thổ phai mờ, gạch azulejo nứt vỡ, những vệt gỉ chảy xuống từ các giá đỡ ban công sắt cũ — tất cả những điều này, về mặt kỹ thuật, là sự xuống cấp. Nó cũng là ghi chép thị giác của một khu phố lao động từ chối bị làm phẳng thành một tấm bưu thiếp.

Thừa nhận rằng, ranh giới giữa sự xuống cấp duyên dáng và sự bỏ bê kết cấu thực sự không phải lúc nào cũng dễ vạch ra. Một mặt tiền không được bảo trì trong bốn mươi năm có thể chuyển từ vẻ đẹp thời gian sang mối nguy hiểm: vữa lỏng có thể rơi xuống đường bên dưới, sắt rỉ có thể hỏng, lớp sơn bong tróc có thể che giấu hơi ẩm đang ăn mòn lớp gạch phía sau. Cư dân có quyền có những tòa nhà an toàn, và hội đồng có nghĩa vụ thực thi quyền đó. Không ai sống dưới một mái hiên đổ nát lại quan tâm đến ký ức cộng đồng của nó.

Dẫu vậy, phản ứng với rủi ro này không nhất thiết phải là sơn lại toàn bộ các khu phố bằng những tông màu được phê duyệt. Trong thực tế, chính sách di sản nhạy bén có dạng hẹp hơn: sửa chữa kết cấu ở nơi thực sự cần, làm sạch thay vì sơn phủ ở nơi bề mặt còn lành lặn, và một sự sẵn lòng để lại những dấu vết của thời tiết ở những nơi những dấu vết đó không gây hại. Naples đã thử nghiệm chính cách tiếp cận này ở các phần của Khu Tây Ban Nha, tài trợ các khoản trợ cấp ổn định mặt tiền mà rõ ràng không yêu cầu chủ sở hữu khôi phục màu sắc ban đầu.

Thừa nhận rằng, một số cư dân muốn có một con phố sạch sẽ hơn. Mong muốn sống sau một mặt tiền mới được trát lại không phải là sự kiêu căng; nó thường là một mong muốn hoàn toàn hợp lý để cảm thấy rằng ngôi nhà của mình được chăm sóc. Sai lầm là giả định rằng sự chăm sóc nhất thiết có nghĩa là sự xóa bỏ.

Một thành phố tô sửa mỗi bức tường đến mức vô hình là một thành phố đã nhầm lẫn bảo trì với phẫu thuật thẩm mỹ. Các khu phố mà những bức tường được phép già đi trước công chúng giữ lại điều mà cảnh quan đường phố đồng nhất hóa không thể mang lại: một cách nhìn về quá khứ mà không phải đến bảo tàng.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a 'what' cleft to redefine what restoration removes?
options: Erasing precisely this long accumulated record a uniformly restored facade quietly does.
What a uniformly restored facade quietly erases is precisely this long, accumulated record.
Precisely a uniformly restored facade erases what this long accumulated record is.
A uniformly restored facade what is precisely erasing this long accumulated record quietly.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … erases is …' places the most important content ('this long, accumulated record') in the emphatic end position.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the key noun phrase appearing after 'is'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'patina' mean in this essay?
options: A type of decorative paint used in heritage districts
The surface effect produced on a material by age, weather, or use, often seen as attractive
A protective coating applied to facades to prevent weathering
A formal classification of building damage used by city councils
mcQuestions.explanation: Patina describes the visible marks that age leaves on a surface — softened colour, oxidation, gentle wear — typically read as desirable rather than damaging.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of an old copper roof turning green, or an old wooden table softening — what is that change called?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'admittedly' correctly as a concession marker?
options: Admittedly, the line between graceful weathering and genuine structural neglect is not always easy to draw.
The line between graceful weathering admittedly not easy to draw and structural neglect is.
Not easy to draw admittedly the line between graceful weathering and structural neglect.
Structural neglect graceful weathering admittedly the line not is easy to draw.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' followed by a complete clause cleanly opens a concession, granting the opposing view a point before the argument continues.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Admittedly,' at the front of a grammatically complete sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a reduced participle clause to compress information about the facade?
options: A facade left untreated for forty years can shade from patina into hazard.
A facade which forty years untreated left can shade from patina into hazard.
Untreated forty years a facade left can shade from patina into hazard.
Hazard from patina a facade for forty years untreated left can shade.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'left untreated for forty years' is a reduced passive relative clause: it stands in for 'that has been left untreated for forty years' and modifies 'a facade' compactly.
mcQuestions.hint: The reduced clause follows the noun directly and starts with a past participle ('left').

options: A street where all the buildings have been painted by the same artist
A streetscape in which differences have been smoothed away so that everything looks the same
A street with mixed-use buildings to encourage local commerce
A street that has been milk-pasteurised for hygiene reasons
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Homogenised' means made uniform; a homogenised streetscape has lost the visible differences between its buildings.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'homogenised' as 'made the same throughout' — what would that do to a street's appearance?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'a working-class neighbourhood that has refused to be flattened into a postcard'?
options: A neighbourhood whose residents refuse to send postcards about it
A working-class district that has resisted being smoothed into a tourist image
A district that has banned the printing of postcards depicting it
A neighbourhood that has been turned into a flat, two-dimensional design
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Flattened into a postcard' is a metaphor: the district has refused to lose its real texture in order to become a pretty tourist image.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean to 'flatten' something complex into a simple image?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds what the building suffers?
options: Damp eats into the masonry behind the peeling paint.
Peeling paint can hide damp that is eating into the masonry behind it.
Behind the peeling paint masonry eats into damp.
Masonry behind the paint peeling damp eats into.
mcQuestions.explanation: The sentence uses an active 'is eating' inside a relative clause to foreground the danger, with 'damp' as agent and 'masonry' as the object being damaged — preserving the cause-and-effect logic the essay needs.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps the cause ('damp') and the effect ('masonry') in a clear, readable order?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to legislate aesthetic uniformity' mean?
options: To pass laws requiring buildings to share a uniform look
To ban any kind of legislation about how buildings should look
To negotiate quietly with property owners about colour choices
To distribute uniform paintbrushes to municipal workers
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Legislate' means to make law; legislating aesthetic uniformity means using law to enforce a single shared visual standard.
mcQuestions.hint: Break the phrase into its parts: legislate (make law) + aesthetic (visual) + uniformity (sameness).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the response to this risk does not have to be the wholesale repainting of entire districts.
On the contrary, there is no risk at all.
Furthermore the wholesale repainting is needed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the conceded point ('weathering can become neglect') and pivots back to the main argument that wholesale repainting is the wrong response.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and immediately reasserts the writer's position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All buildings older than fifty years should be left untouched.
Heritage policy should fix structural problems but resist erasing the visible signs of age.
Residents should not be allowed to repaint their own homes without council approval.
Faded facades are always more beautiful than restored ones.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues for a narrow, surgical policy: repair what is dangerous, leave what is merely weathered. This is stated explicitly in paragraph four.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the description of 'what sensitive heritage policy looks like, in practice' in paragraph four.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Old buildings look way cooler when they're a bit messed up, that's just a fact.
Honestly nobody wants to live in a building that's falling apart, but we should still keep some old vibes.
Sensitive heritage policy distinguishes between superficial weathering and underlying structural risk, intervening only where the latter is present.
Cities just need to chill out about repainting everything and let walls do their thing.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('superficial weathering', 'underlying structural risk', 'intervening only where') and avoids contractions or colloquial intensifiers.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid contractions, slang ('vibes', 'chill'), and intensifiers like 'way' or 'honestly'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to retouch every wall into invisibility' mean in the conclusion?
options: To paint walls in colours that physically disappear at night
To repair walls so thoroughly that no trace of their history can be seen
To install hidden cameras in every facade
To photograph walls and digitally remove imperfections
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Retouch into invisibility' is a metaphor: the wall is still there, but everything visually distinctive about its age and history has been erased.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean to 'retouch' a photograph? Apply the same logic to a wall.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: Districts whose walls are allowed to age in public retain something the homogenised streetscape cannot offer.
Districts retain something the homogenised streetscape whose walls are allowed to age in public cannot offer.
Whose walls districts allowed to age in public retain something the homogenised streetscape cannot offer.
Cannot offer the homogenised streetscape districts whose walls retain in public allowed to age something.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'whose walls are allowed to age in public' is a defining relative clause specifying which districts the writer means.
mcQuestions.hint: The relative clause starts with 'whose' and follows the noun it describes ('Districts').

options: A type of structural cement used in southern European construction
A traditional Portuguese decorative ceramic tile, typically blue and white
A municipal grant for repainting old buildings
A heritage organisation based in Lisbon
mcQuestions.explanation: Azulejos are glazed ceramic tiles, often blue and white, that decorate facades and interiors throughout Portugal and Spain.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of the famous blue-and-white tiles that cover many Lisbon facades.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best concludes by contrasting two visions of the city?
options: In conclusion, both restoration and weathering have their place.
Therefore, all walls must be left in their natural state forever.
A city that retouches every wall into invisibility is one that has confused maintenance with cosmetic surgery.
To sum up, walls are important to cities and we should think about them.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C is a sharp, specific contrast that names exactly what the writer believes goes wrong when restoration becomes cosmetic — a strong rhetorical close.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a memorable, specific comparison rather than a vague summary?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best captures the essay's view of weathered facades?
options: Visual evidence of neglect that responsible owners must address
A liability for tourists who might trip on falling render
An accumulated record of the neighbourhood's history, valuable in its own right
A symbol of poverty that property values should overcome
mcQuestions.explanation: Throughout, the essay treats weathered facades as carrying real informational and historical value — 'the texture of lived time' rather than mere decay.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at how the essay describes what restoration 'erases'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a building, street, or neighbourhood near you that has visibly aged. If a council grant offered to restore its facade tomorrow, would you want the work done — or would you want some of the marks of time preserved? What would you ask the restorers to leave alone?
